name: Credne
description: Credne, in Irish mythology, is a skilled craftsman and one of the three gods of the Tuatha Dé Danann known for their artistry. He is primarily associated with metalworking, particularly bronze and copper, and is revered as a divine smith. His expertise was crucial to the Tuatha Dé Danann, providing them with weapons and armor essential for their battles. Credne's skill was not merely functional; his creations were imbued with magical properties and artistry, reflecting his divine status. He is often mentioned alongside Goibniu, the divine smith, and Luchtaine, the divine carpenter, forming a triad of artisan deities whose combined talents were vital to the Tuatha Dé Danann's success. While not as prominent as some other deities, Credne's importance lies in his representation of the skilled craftsmanship and technological prowess that underpinned the mythological world of ancient Ireland. His role highlights the value placed on skilled labor and the divine origins attributed to essential crafts.
subtitle: The Divine Metalworker

keyMyths.title: The Silver Arm of Nuada
keyMyths.description: During the first battle of Mag Tuired, Nuada Airgetlám, King of the Tuatha Dé Danann, lost his arm. To restore his status as king, he needed a replacement. Credne, along with Dian Cécht, the physician, crafted a silver arm for Nuada. Dian Cécht provided the medical expertise, while Credne provided the craftsmanship and metalworking skill. This new arm allowed Nuada to regain his position as king, showcasing Credne's crucial role in the affairs of the gods. The silver arm itself became a powerful symbol of kingship and restoration.
keyMyths.source: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired

keyMyths.title: Weapons for the Tuatha Dé Danann
keyMyths.description: Credne played a vital role in equipping the Tuatha Dé Danann with weapons and armor. He forged swords, spears, and shields, ensuring that the gods were well-prepared for battle. His craftsmanship was not merely functional; the weapons he crafted were imbued with magical properties, enhancing their effectiveness. These weapons were crucial in the battles against the Fir Bolg and the Fomorians, demonstrating Credne's essential contribution to the Tuatha Dé Danann's victories. His creations were considered divine artifacts.

keyMyths.title: The Triad of Divine Artisans
keyMyths.description: Credne often appears in conjunction with Goibniu and Luchtaine, forming a powerful triad of divine artisans. Goibniu, the smith, Luchtaine, the carpenter, and Credne, the metalworker, each possessed unique skills that complemented one another. Together, they were responsible for creating the tools, weapons, and structures necessary for the Tuatha Dé Danann's society. Their combined talents represent the importance of craftsmanship and the interconnectedness of different skills in ancient Irish culture. This triad highlights the value placed on skilled labor and the divine origins attributed to essential crafts.
